nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater peat swamps and slow-moving forest streams; water strongly stained by humic acids, dense leaf-litter substrate, submerged roots and branches, minimal mineral content

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, cryptocorynes, floating plants to diffuse light)
dark fine sand or gravel substrate

diet.notes: Micropredator in nature, feeding on small insects, worms, crustaceans, and zooplankton. In the aquarium accepts quality micro-pellets and fine flake, but benefits greatly from daily offerings of small live or frozen foods — Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae — to maintain best coloration and breeding condition.

goodWith: other Desmopuntius species (D. hexazona, D. pentazona, D. johorensis)
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-feeding tankmates that outcompete for food
hard-water fish incompatible with soft acidic conditions

summary: A small, strikingly patterned Bornean blackwater barb. The distinctive dark rhomboid blotches on an orange-red body inspired both the “snakeskin” and “rhombo” trade names. Requires soft, acidic water and a large school; intermediate care due to water-chemistry sensitivity.
careNotes:
The Snakeskin Barb is endemic to the peat swamp forests and blackwater streams of western and central Kalimantan (Indonesian Borneo), where water is heavily stained by humic and tannic acids, pH can drop below 4.0, and mineral content is extremely low. In the aquarium it is more forgiving than its natural habitat suggests — a pH of 5.0–7.0 and very soft water (gH 1–8, kH 0–4) is the practical target, with temperature held in the 22–26°C range for everyday keeping. Tannins from Indian almond leaves, dried oak leaves, or alder cones are strongly recommended to acidify and stain the water, and peat filtration is a traditional method for achieving the necessary chemistry.

This is a schooling species by nature; groups of eight or more are strongly advised — fewer fish show increased shyness, reduced color, and stress. A tank of at least 80 cm in length with subdued lighting, dense planting, a dark substrate, and abundant driftwood best replicates the forest-stream biotope and brings out the species’ vivid orange and black patterning.

The Snakeskin Barb was described as Puntius rhomboocellatus by Koumans in 1940 and was for many decades traded and kept under that name. Kottelat (2013) erected the genus Desmopuntius to accommodate a clade of small Southeast Asian barbs including this species and close relatives such as D. hexazona and D. pentazona; the current valid binomial is Desmopuntius rhomboocellatus.

Water quality must be excellent; the species is sensitive to elevated nitrates and organic waste. Biweekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Diet should be varied: supplement prepared micro-pellets and fine flake with daily servings of small live or frozen invertebrates (Daphnia, brine shrimp nauplii, bloodworms) to maintain optimal color and condition.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care. Conditioning groups on live and frozen foods in soft, acidic, warm water (around 26–28°C, pH 5.0–6.0) will trigger spawning. A separate breeding tank with plastic grass matting, marbles, or a fine mesh over the base prevents adults from consuming eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming after 3–4 days and require infusoria or commercial first-foods initially, progressing to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms. Breeding is considered moderately difficult in the hobby due to strict water-chemistry requirements and the need for very well-conditioned adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Desmopuntius rhomboocellatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (50–55 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–90 ppm ≈ gH 1–8), minimum tank base (80 × 30 cm), native range (Kapuas, Kepayang, Barito, Kahajan systems, southern Kalimantan), blackwater biotope description, diet, social requirements (groups of 8–10+), compatible tankmates, and breeding notes (eggs hatch 24–36 h, fry free-swimming 3–4 days).

additional.name: FishBase — Desmopuntius rhomboocellatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (8.8 cm TL; note this appears to be an outlier — SeriouslyFish 55 mm SL and trade consensus of ~5 cm preferred), temperature (23–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5 per FishBase — narrower than SeriouslyFish; SeriouslyFish range used as more conservative for specialist blackwater requirements), hardness (to 15 dH), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes, subfamily Smiliogastrinae), habitat (blackwater streams, Kalimantan), IUCN Least Concern (2020).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Top 10 Energetic Barbs
additional.notes: Trade perspective; confirmed size (2–2.5 inches / 5–6 cm), peaceful temperament in community with similarly sized active fish, group of 6–10 recommended, blackwater Borneo origin, body description (“tannish-orange body with black vertical markings similar to ball python patterning”). Minimum 20-gallon tank cited; 80 L used per SeriouslyFish dimensions.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Desmopuntius rhomboocellatus
additional.notes: Confirmed endemic distribution (western and central Kalimantan, Borneo), Kottelat 2013 genus-level reclassification from Puntius, subfamily Smiliogastrinae placement, common name origin from rhomboid ocellus markings on flanks.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Rhombo Barb care guide
additional.notes: Additional trade source; confirmed KH 4–10, lifespan 4–6 years, sexual dimorphism (males slimmer and more intensely coloured, females broader), breeding difficulty (strict water chemistry requirements), fry feeding progression (infusoria → baby brine shrimp → microworms).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; no parental care. Requires well-conditioned adults, soft acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0, 26–28°C), and a bare breeding tank with egg-protection mesh or marbles. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry free-swimming after 3–4 days. Infusoria then baby brine shrimp as first foods.
